3.happening: Measure the matrix — how many rows.
3.why: We need the bounds to test neighbours.
3.invariant: The matrix dimensions are fixed for the whole run.

4.happening: Measure how many columns each row has.
4.why: Column count bounds our left/right moves.
4.invariant: Every row has the same width.

5.why: This grid — not the input — is what we fill and return.
5.invariant: dist will end up holding every cell's distance to the nearest zero.

6.happening: Loop over each row to build a matching distance row.
6.why: dist must mirror the matrix's shape.
6.invariant: Each dist row will hold exactly {cols} entries.

7.happening: Fill the row with -1 — 'not reached yet'.
7.why: -1 marks cells the wave has not touched, so we can tell them apart from a real distance of 0.

8.why: We must remember which cells are queued or served so none is stamped twice.

9.happening: Loop over each row to build a matching state row.
9.why: state must mirror the grid's shape.
9.invariant: Each state row holds exactly {cols} zeros.

10.happening: Fill it with zeros — every cell starts unreached.
10.invariant: No cell is marked before the search begins.

11.happening: Reset the current-row marker to -1 (no active cell).
11.why: There's an active cell only while serving the queue.

12.why: Same — nothing is being served yet.
12.invariant: cur_r and cur_c together locate the active cell.

13.happening: List the four orthogonal moves (down, up, right, left).
13.why: Distance is measured along edges, so the wave spreads only orthogonally.
13.invariant: Only these four directions are ever tried.

14.why: The queue holds discovered cells waiting to spread the wave.
14.invariant: The queue always holds the current frontier.

15.happening: Scan every row looking for zeros.
15.why: Every zero is a starting point of the search.
15.invariant: All zeros are found before the wave starts.

16.happening: Scan every column of this row.
16.why: We must check each cell to find the sources.
16.invariant: Each cell is inspected exactly once here.

17.happening: Cell ({r},{c}) is a zero — a source of the wave.
17.why: A zero is distance 0 from itself, so it seeds the search.

17.happening: Cell ({r},{c}) is a one — not a source.
17.why: Ones get their distance later, when the wave reaches them.

18.why: A zero's nearest zero is itself.

19.why: So it is never queued a second time.
19.invariant: A cell enters the queue at most once.

20.why: ALL zeros start in the queue together — that shared start is what makes this multi-source BFS.
20.invariant: The wave expands from every zero simultaneously.

21.happening: The queue holds {len(queue)} cell(s) — keep spreading the wave.
21.why: Every queued cell can still reach unvisited neighbours.
21.invariant: Cells are served in non-decreasing distance order.

21.happening: The queue is empty — every cell has been reached.
21.why: No frontier remains, so all distances are final.

22.happening: Take the cell at the FRONT of the queue.
22.why: Serving oldest-first guarantees a cell is reached by the shortest wave first, so its stamped distance is the minimum.
22.invariant: The served cell's distance is already final.

23.why: We need its coordinates to reach neighbours.

24.why: Row and column together locate the active cell.
24.invariant: cur_r, cur_c now point at the cell spreading the wave.

25.happening: Mark the active cell ({cur_r},{cur_c}) fully served (2).
25.why: Its distance is locked and its neighbours are about to be checked.

26.happening: Try each of the four directions from this cell.
26.why: To find adjacent cells the wave has not yet reached.
26.invariant: All four neighbours are examined before moving on.

27.why: The direction's row offset added to the active cell.
27.invariant: nr may be off-grid — we check next.

28.why: The direction's column offset added to the active cell.
28.invariant: nc may be off-grid — we check next.

29.when: nr >= 0 && nr < rows && nc >= 0 && nc < cols && state[nr][nc] == 0
29.happening: Neighbour ({nr},{nc}) is in-bounds and unreached — the wave arrives here now.
29.why: Reaching it now, from a distance-{dist[cur_r][cur_c]} cell, gives it the smallest possible distance.
29.invariant: A cell is stamped exactly once, with its minimum distance.

29.happening: Neighbour ({nr},{nc}) is off-grid or already reached — skip it.
29.why: A cell already reached has an equal-or-smaller distance; re-stamping would never improve it.

30.why: It is one edge farther from the nearest zero than the cell that reached it.
30.invariant: Each ring of the wave carries a distance one greater than the last.

31.why: Claim it before queueing so it can't be stamped twice.
31.invariant: Discovered cells are waiting in the queue.

32.happening: Add the neighbour to the back of the queue.
32.why: It waits behind everything discovered earlier, which keeps the wave expanding ring by ring.

33.happening: Every cell holds its distance to the nearest zero — return the grid.
33.why: The wave reached every cell exactly once, at its minimum distance.
33.invariant: The returned grid is the answer.

init.happening: Setting up the distance grid, the state tracker, and an empty queue.
init.why: We fill a fresh grid rather than overwriting the input.

loop.happening: Scanning for sources, or spreading the wave from the queue.
loop.why: First find every zero, then let the frontier expand outward.
loop.invariant: Cells are served in non-decreasing distance order.

check.happening: Deciding whether a cell is a source, or whether a neighbour is reachable and new.
check.why: Only zeros seed the search; only unreached in-bounds cells get stamped.
check.invariant: Each cell is stamped at most once.

update.happening: Stamping a cell with its distance and marking it part of the wave.
update.why: dist and state must reflect the growing frontier.
update.invariant: A stamped cell keeps its minimum distance.

move.happening: Serving the oldest cell and reaching toward its neighbours.
move.why: The wave spreads to adjacent unreached cells.

return.happening: The wave has reached everywhere — returning the distance grid.
return.invariant: Every cell holds its true distance to the nearest zero.
